title: Why gambling on ROBLOX was fun in 2022
summary: A look back at an old project idea, how it came to life and my favorite takeaways

sections.heading: Background
sections.body: In August of 2022, a high school friend of mine named Steven talked about how rich in robux he was back then and how people would use a technique called [sniping](https://www.reddit.com/r/roblox/comments/asx0ez/should_trade_snipers_be_banned/), which is essentailly using a bot for arbitrage of ["limited"](https://roblox.fandom.com/wiki/Limited_item) high value cosmetic items on ROBLOX. Around this time I was fresh out of my first engineering internship and was eager to build. The idea of bots and the ROBLOX economy was pretty interesting to me.

sections.heading: Fast-Forward
sections.body:
After Stevie dropped some ball knowledge in ROBLOX exploits and I did some due diligence of my own I learned a couple of things:

1. There is a way to get [instant access to ANY ROBLOX account](https://roblox.fandom.com/wiki/.ROBLOSECURITY)

2. There are (were) API endpoints for managing groups in ROBLOX which enabled the payout of robux to several people at a time

3. With an [X-CSRF-Token](https://security.stackexchange.com/questions/154410/how-does-x-csrf-token-work) and a Security Cookie, you can authenticate an account programmatically

sections.heading: Let's Make it Rain
sections.body: The idea became clear. What if we could make a Discord bot that controlled a Roblox group treasury, distrubuted real virtual currency, and ran high intensity community-style events for live users? And so Robux Rain was born.

sections.heading: A quick moment for thoughts
sections.body:
I decided not to overwhelm this post with code and technical details. That version can exist another time. For now, I wanted this to be more about where the idea came from, what made it exciting, and what it felt like watching people actually use it.

I think the best part of starting any project is the moment your brain switches from “this would be cool” to “wait, this is actually possible.” You start breaking the idea down into smaller pieces: “I just need to figure out this one part,” then the next part, then the next. Eventually, the thing that only existed in your head is on someone else’s screen.

That was the real takeaway for me. The code mattered, but the best part was seeing my friends enjoy something I brought to life end to end. It reminded me that a project does not have to be serious to be meaningful. Sometimes the fun, chaotic ideas are the ones that teach you the most because you actually care enough to finish them.

sections.heading: What about the Gambling???
sections.body:
Right! So around the time there was (AND IS) a popular site called [Bloxflip.com](https://bloxflip.com/) which I kid you not is a gambling site for robux; make whatever conclusions about the demographic of users you will. Is it illegal? Probably not, its still up as of 10:30 PM 5/27/2026. Is it against ROBLOX TOS? Absolutely. 

So, in the spirit of robux, gambling, and community, I made a spin on the project: What if instead of simply fixed amounts, we made the bot pay out a variable amount from a prizepool in a giveaway style fashion? It makes for both an exciting experience and incentive to gamble to multiply the high amounts already rewarded or grow the pennies leftover.

sections.caption: there is a strong correlation between sled height and stress

sections.heading: Cool Moments
sections.body: Feedback loops are pretty satisfying and useful for iterating. I made sure to have the bot announce in the channel when someone was paid out so seing those messages pop up was great. Although sometimes people would win and be upset they couldn't claim thier prize due to Roblox's 2 week probation on becoming a group member, so no money :(

sections.heading: Slightly less cool moments
sections.body:
Maybe you'd expect the highest payout story to be interesting but it is actually very boring. Although it was around 1500 R$, the winner bought a [Dr. House](https://www.rolimons.com/item/91950361017105) cosmetic for only 90 R$

Many of the risktakers on the other hand were washed by Bloxflip...

sections.heading: Wrap  Up
sections.body:
Whenever I'm asked why I wanted to go into CS or tech, I always think back to this project.

Looking back, Robux Rain wasn't really about ROBLOX, gambling, or even the code itself. The part that stuck with me was watching something that started as a random idea between friends turn into an experience that other people genuinely enjoyed. A few API calls, some Discord commands, and a weekend of building ended up creating moments that brought people together, sparked conversations, and gave a community something fun to rally around.

I think that's what has always interested me most about technology. Not just building software for the sake of building software, but creating tools, platforms, and systems that help people connect with one another. Whether it's a Discord bot, a website, or something much larger, the underlying idea feels the same: using technology to bring people together, make communities stronger, and create experiences that wouldn't exist otherwise.

Years later, that goal hasn't changed much. The projects are different, the code is better, and the problems are more complex, but I'm still chasing the same feeling I had when I watched people use Robux Rain for the first time: taking an idea that only existed in my head and turning it into something that makes a community a little more connected than it was before.
